top_comment.author: TsviBT
top_comment.text: IME a lot of people's stated reasons for thinking AGI is near involve mistaken reasoning and those mistakes can be discussed without revealing capabilities ideas: https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/sTDfraZab47KiRMmT/views-on-when-agi-comes-and-on-strategy-to-reduce
top_comment.claim: The counterclaim is: IME a lot of people's stated reasons for thinking AGI is near involve mistaken reasoning and those mistakes can be discussed without revealing capabilities ideas: https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/sTDfraZab47KiRMmT/views-on-when-agi-comes-an…

crux.crux_question: Can the case that AGI is not near be argued publicly without handing out capability-relevant insights (a 'roadmap'), or does spelling out missing capabilities inevitably accelerate progress?
crux.evidence_post: One person's list of capabilities AGI still lacks is another person's roadmap, which is a reason to be cautious about publicly discussing why AGI might not be near.
crux.evidence_comment: TsviBT argues that many stated reasons for thinking AGI is near rest on mistaken reasoning that can be rebutted without revealing any capabilities ideas.

The main claim is: If we were trying to get to the moon for the first time, understanding Newtonian mechanics would indeed be quite useful!

What we needed throughout time ------------------------------ Taking a step back, these problems were downstream of much of the development of science and technology since the start of the industrial revolution.
